note: Ladder rung 6: Continuum / CacheTTL (arXiv 2511.02230), gap-aware PROTECTION, our faithful reconstruction of the mechanism. This closes the head-to-head: the third published policy is now evaluated, not represented by the IdleTTL strawman. Continuum keeps a session's KV alive through tool-call gaps and releases it only when the session is predicted done. Disclosed substitution: gap estimated from session-level recency against a swept horizon, where the paper predicts it from workflow (a hint); the hint version is the perfect-signal upper bound.
provenance: Committed with results=TBD BEFORE the run (strict protocol; git timestamp proves prediction precedes number).

prediction_registered_before_run.degeneracy_anchors_hard: gap_ms=0 protects nothing => exactly LRU (160.5); gap_ms -> very large protects every active session's whole prefix => LRU over the protected set (also ~160.5). Both hard anchors. Any Continuum win must be at an INTERIOR horizon.
prediction_registered_before_run.mechanism_prediction: Continuum should barely beat LRU at this high-pressure cell, for a specific reason the ladder already surfaced: the IdleTTL rung showed idle != dead because append-mostly prefixes are re-read in full each turn, so the ENTIRE prefix of an active session is 'returning', and recency already protects recently-touched blocks. Gap-aware protection at session granularity therefore overlaps heavily with what recency already does; it adds value only for blocks that are idle-past-recency but still returning, a thin slice on this workload. Registered expectation: Continuum's best interior horizon lands within a couple of points of LRU (158-162%), NOT a decisive win, and its horizon sweep may be near-degenerate (optimum at or close to an extreme). This CONFIRMS Continuum's premise (which IdleTTL already showed: idle-time signals must not be used to evict) while finding its protection mechanism adds little over recency here.
prediction_registered_before_run.regime_prediction: If Continuum helps anywhere it is the critical band (cap/T ~0.45-0.53), same as retirement and pricing, giving the three-regime map a third consistent instance. At high pressure churn absorbs protection; at slack LRU is already optimal.
prediction_registered_before_run.falsifiable_edges: (1) a degeneracy anchor != 160.5 is a bug; (2) a decisive high-pressure win (>5 points) would REFUTE the 'recency already protects the returning prefix' reasoning and would be the most interesting outcome; (3) a non-degenerate interior optimum that beats LRU in the band would show gap-aware protection is a real, separable signal.

horizon_sweep_at_cap_0_33.reading: Continuum is LRU to within 0.2 points at EVERY horizon. Both degeneracy anchors confirmed at 160.5 (gap->0 protects nothing, gap->inf protects the whole active prefix). Gap-aware protection adds nothing at high pressure because idle != dead: recency already protects the returning prefix, so session-level protection overlaps it.

across_pressure_gap_60000.note: absolute capacity-miss reduction vs LRU (negative = Continuum MISSES MORE); caps = int(cap/T * 3,698,880)

results.best_case_is_degenerate: Continuum's best achievable number at any pressure is LRU, reached at gap->0. At a real tool-call-scale horizon (60s) it does NOT help and actively HURTS in the band and slack (-22% at 0.49, -62% at 0.53): protecting sessions inside the horizon over-retains stale prefixes, evicting the live blocks that are actually about to be reused. This is the same converges-to-degenerate signature IdleTTL showed: a single-signal policy whose best case is its mechanism-off case is evidence the signal is wrong for the workload.

prediction_outcome: CONFIRMED, and sharper than registered. Degeneracy anchors confirmed (both 160.5). Mechanism prediction ('barely beats LRU, near-degenerate') was right in direction and stronger in fact: Continuum does not beat LRU at ANY horizon or pressure; its best case IS LRU and off-degenerate it hurts. Regime prediction ('if it helps, in the band') REFUTED informatively: it does not help in the band, it hurts MOST there. So gap-aware protection is the one mechanism that is purely redundant-to-harmful on this workload, unlike retirement (helps in band) and pricing (helps in band, fragile).
fairness_caveat_inference_only: This is the INFERENCE-ONLY reconstruction: gaps are estimated from session-level recency, which is exactly the signal that turns out redundant. The paper's contribution is PREDICTING gaps from workflow structure (a hint). A workflow-hint Continuum that correctly separates 'session in a tool call, will return' from 'session finished' could target protection where recency cannot, and is NOT evaluated here. The honest claim is narrow and fair: inference-only gap-aware protection (recency-estimated gaps) provides no benefit and can backfire on agentic coding traces; the hint-driven version is future work and the natural next rung, exactly as for WA-LRU's graph-driven variant.
confirms_vs_tests: Like IdleTTL, this CONFIRMS Continuum's premise (idle != dead, so idle-time must not drive eviction) while finding its inference-only protection mechanism does not separate from recency here. It does NOT test the workflow-hint version, which is where Continuum's real contribution lives.
